FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 3 follow-up (2019-2021), ages 11-14 years, 9989 participants from 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Cross-sectional logistic regression analysis with adjusted odds ratios (OR 2.24, 95% CI 1.22-4.10 for transgender; OR 1.91, 95% CI 1.05-3.47 for gender-questioning)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization is the outcome; gender identity is the IV. No separately-measured smartphone or social media use variable is paired with an individual-trait-level variable. Cyberbullying alone without a SM/phone variable does not qualify per criteria §2.4

notes: FT-EC2 exclusion: Cyberbullying alone without separately-measured smartphone or social media variable

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 3 follow-up (2019-2021), ages 11-15 years, 10,092 participants from 21 geographically diverse U.S. sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with descriptive statistics and Chi-square and t-test comparisons of social media platform use across demographic characteristics

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper describes prevalence and patterns of social media platform use (TikTok 67.1%, Instagram 66.0%, YouTube 64.7%, Snapchat 60.3%) but does not pair SM use as IV or DV with any individual-trait-level variable (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, etc.). This is purely descriptive prevalence reporting, not an analysis linking SM to an individual-trait outcome

notes: FT-EC2 exclusion: Social media use reported as prevalence only, not paired with individual-trait-level outcome or predictor in any analysis

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, prospective cohort Year 2 (2018-2020) and Year 3 follow-up, ages 11-12 years at Year 2, 8006 participants from 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Prospective logistic regression analysis with adjusted odds ratios reported for screen time modalities predicting substance use experimentation one year later

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use (AOR 1.20 per hour, 95% CI 1.14-1.26 for any substance use), texting (AOR 1.18, 95% CI 1.12-1.24), and video chatting (AOR 1.09, 95% CI 1.03-1.16) as qualifying SM/phone modalities measured as IVs; substance use experimentation (alcohol, nicotine, cannabis) as individual-trait-level behavioral/health DVs

FT_IC3.evidence: Prospective logistic regression with numeric results: social media use associated with higher odds of alcohol (AOR 1.15, 95% CI 1.10-1.21), nicotine (AOR 1.23, 95% CI 1.14-1.32), and cannabis (AOR 1.22, 95% CI 1.13-1.33) experimentation in Year 3

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple numeric inferential results reported: texting (AOR 1.14 alcohol, 1.16 nicotine, 1.18 cannabis); video chatting (AOR 1.14 nicotine, 1.13 cannabis); total screen time (AOR 1.01-1.03)

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design but SM/phone variables are on IV side of equation, not DV-only; therefore EC7 exclusion does not apply
notes: Paper meets all criteria. Qualifies for inclusion. Digital social connections (social media, texting, video chatting) prospectively associated with early adolescent substance use experimentation
